coredata.dc:title: Molecular dynamics simulations on chitosan/graphene nanocomposites as anticancer drug delivery using systems

Mazloom-Jalali, Azin
coredata.dc:description: Several chitosan (CS) drug delivery systems (DDSs) were designed by molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. The systems were composed of pure graphene (G), P-doped (GP) or N-doped (GN) graphene nanosheets for the anticancer drug cyclophosphamide (CP) with the aim of discovering the most appropriate drug carrier. Furthermore, the temperature influence was investigated on the systems characteristics through performing the simulations at four different temperatures including 25, 35, 45 and 55 °C. The diffusion coefficients and mean square displacements (MSDs) were boosted with the temperature increase. At 55 °C, the CS-G-CP exhibited the greatest diffusion coefficient (0.0658 × 10–5 cm2/s) but the CS-GN-CP had the smallest diffusion coefficient of 0.0553 × 10–5 cm2/s and the CS-GP-CP illustrated a medium amount (0.0595 × 10–5 cm2/s) indicating the most controlled/sustained drug diffusion could occur in the CS-GN-CP which could allow the most efficient drug delivery. Moreover, to evaluate the effects of PEG chains and VC molecule on the drug delivery capacity of the CS-GN-CP cell, they were added to the this system and the CS-GN-CP-PEG2-VC was nominated as the best DDS working at 35 °C (close to the human body temperature) due to it had a relatively high drug loading capacity as well as suitable CP diffusion coefficient and FV, FFV values.

head.ce:title: Molecular dynamics simulations on chitosan/graphene nanocomposites as anticancer drug delivery using systems

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Several chitosan (CS) drug delivery systems (DDSs) were designed by molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. The systems were composed of pure graphene (G), P-doped (GP) or N-doped (GN) graphene nanosheets for the anticancer drug cyclophosphamide (CP) with the aim of discovering the most appropriate drug carrier. Furthermore, the temperature influence was investigated on the systems characteristics through performing the simulations at four different temperatures including 25, 35, 45 and 55 °C. The diffusion coefficients and mean square displacements (MSDs) were boosted with the temperature increase. At 55 °C, the CS-G-CP exhibited the greatest diffusion coefficient (0.0658 × 10–5 cm2/s) but the CS-GN-CP had the smallest diffusion coefficient of 0.0553 × 10–5 cm2/s and the CS-GP-CP illustrated a medium amount (0.0595 × 10–5 cm2/s) indicating the most controlled/sustained drug diffusion could occur in the CS-GN-CP which could allow the most efficient drug delivery. Moreover, to evaluate the effects of PEG chains and VC molecule on the drug delivery capacity of the CS-GN-CP cell, they were added to the this system and the CS-GN-CP-PEG2-VC was nominated as the best DDS working at 35 °C (close to the human body temperature) due to it had a relatively high drug loading capacity as well as suitable CP diffusion coefficient and FV, FFV values.

MD simulations were performed on chitosan nanocomposites as drug delivery systems and the effect of temperature was investigated on the characteristics of these systems. The CS-GN-CP exhibited the lowest diffusion coefficient for the CP drug. The CS-GN-CP-PEG2-VC was nominated as the best drug delivery system at 308.15 K
Image, graphical abstract
.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Constant number of particles, volume and temperature
Constant number of particles, pressure and temperature

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is known as the second principal death cause worldwide so that 1 death out of 6 deaths is caused by cancer. It has been reported that approximately 14 million cancer deaths were occurred around the world in 2012 and most of these deaths were related to liver, stomach, lung, colorectal, oesophageal and breast cancers [1]. Also, it has been found that in 2018, approximately 9.6 million deaths have globally been occurred due to various cancers [2]. Thus, it is required to design novel anticancer drug delivery systems (DDSs) to substantially enhance the cancer treatment efficacy [3–8]. So far, numerous nanomaterials have been investigated as nano-carriers to deliver therapeutic agents so that they are different in their shape, size and chemical composition which include metals, metal oxides, dendrimers, liposomes, micelles, polymer matrixes and carbon nanomaterials (graphene, carbon nanotubes and fullerenes) [9–19]. Among these drug carriers, graphene has received great attraction to use them in delivery of therapeutics. The graphene nanosheets contain sp2-hybridized carbon atoms organized as a two-dimensional honeycomb network [20]. In fact, graphene is composed of some layers with π-conjugated six-membered rings that form an aromatic and planar macromolecule. The planar structure endows with outstanding ability to immobilize numerous compounds such as biomolecules, drug molecules, metals and fluorescent cells as probes [21]. Graphene has a unique structure, geometry and physicochemical properties including extraordinary specific surface area, Young's modulus, fracture strength, thermal and electrical conductivity, charge carrier mobility and biocompatibility [22]. Such properties allow graphene to be used as a perfect material for extensive purposes including nanoelectronics, catalysis, quantum physics, energy production, biomaterials engineering as well as nanocomposites [23]. Graphene and its composites can be utilized in nanomedicine to develop new kinds of drug nano-carriers, biosensors and probes for imaging cells and biomolecules [24]. Moreover, the properly modified graphene serves as an outstanding material for delivery of anticancer drugs and genes, bioimaging, biosensor, antimicrobial application, tissue engineering and cell culture [25]. In drug delivery, 200% loading capacity can be achieved using graphene nanostructures and this is significantly superior to those of other DDSs and nanoparticles [26]. Indeed, graphene derivatives with their beneficial properties are appeared as competitive DDSs with ability to be employed in local, systemic and targeting delivery platforms. The graphene nanomaterials as nano-therapeutic carriers can transport diverse therapeutic substances such as small drug molecules, genes, DNA, antibodies as well as proteins. It is notable that the pristine graphene is very hydrophobic, weakly water dispersible and necessitates using surfactants and surface modification for application in biomedicine and pharmacology [27,28].

Cyclophosphamide (CP, Fig. S1) or cytophosphane is an anticancer drug that acts as a powerful antineoplastic alkylating compound with immunosuppressant activity [29,30]. It belongs to the phosphoramide family [31-38], which is employed in treatment of lymphomas, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, solid tumors as well as osteogenic/soft-tissue sarcomas [39]. CP is used both intravenously and orally but it is inactive till metabolized in liver [40]. In a standard treatment method, high-dose CP (2–3 g/m2) is often utilized by multiple myeloma patients [41]. The CP prodrug is highly metabolized to both active and inactive metabolites. It is mainly transformed into the active phosphoramide mustard compound through the main chain metabolism. Nevertheless, 5–10% of CP is converted to 2-dechloroethylcyclophosphamide (an inactive metabolite) plus chloroacetaldehyde (a by-product) in presence of CYP3A gene through the side chain metabolism [42]. Chloroacetaldehyde leads to hepatotoxicity, neurotoxicity and nephrotoxicity [43].

Currently, the utilization of polymeric carriers for anticancer drugs has gained substantial consideration [44]. It has been found that the drug encapsulation inside polymer chains can protect them against transporters but small sizes of such particulate carriers assist entrance across the biological membranes [45]. Among various polymers, natural biodegradable polymers are commonly applied for encapsulation of anticancer drugs to control drug loading and release rates to afford more successful drug delivery [46]. In other words, biopolymer particles are applied as pharmaceutical formulations for preserving and delivery of bioactive materials [47]. Therefore, natural polymers including polysaccharides are suitable for the preparation of DDSs. It is notable that through the pH control, repulsive and attractive forces occurring between polysaccharide chains and drug molecules enhance the drug delivery efficacy [48].

Chitosan (CS, Fig. S1) is a known as a cationic polysaccharide with pKa=6.5 which is increasingly used in several biomedical and pharmaceutical applications [49]. CS has outstanding characteristics including abundancy, exceptional mucoadhesion, inherent pharmacological properties, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, biodegradability and low immunogenicity [50]. When CS is dissolved in a dilute acidic solution, it becomes a polycationic linear biopolymer [51]. CS was used in the pharmaceutical industry in 1990s and resulted in producing more efficient therapeutic systems by both industrial and academic researchers [52]. The amino groups of CS act as the reactive site for attachment of various groups. In addition, the cationic character of CS is ascribed to the existence of amino groups thus it is recognized as an amino polysaccharide. CS is a copolymer that contains d-glucosamine as well as N-acetyl-d-glucosamine units with two hydroxyl (OH) groups plus one amino (NH2) group in every glycosidic repeating unit [53]. Protonation of its amino groups at low pH makes CS a polycationic species with increased solubility. In addition to the application of CS as a drug delivery system, it is used in biomedical areas including tissue engineering, slimming and wound dressing. This is due to CS is achieved from renewable and nontoxic resources which acts as a biocompatible, biodegradable and active biomaterial. Other valuable biological features of CS such as mucoadhesion, controlled drug delivery, in situ gelation, transfection, colon targeting, inhibition of efflux pump and permeation increase have been associated with the presence of amino groups in its structure [54]. Furthermore, bio-adhesiveness of CS leads to its attachment to both soft and hard tissues that causes its application in dentistry, surgery, ophthalmology and orthopaedics [55].

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are commonly conducted to recognize various changes taking place at the molecular level in complex membranes [56,57]. The MD simulations discover the inter- and intra-molecular interactions between drug molecules and polymer vehicles in nanocomposite polymer delivery platforms [58]. It is worth mentioning that rare computational simulations have hitherto been done on the anticancer drug delivery using polymer nanocomposite drug delivery systems [59-62]. Also, most researchers have experimentally investigated anticancer drug delivery using polymeric composites and there are little computational efforts on this topic. As a result, it was intriguing as a novel idea to accomplish MD simulations on the delivery of the cyclophosphamide (CP) anticancer drug molecules by means of chitosan (CS) polymer nanocomposites composed of graphene (G), N-doped (GN) or P-doped (GP) graphene nanosheets. Herein, the diffusion behaviors of CP molecules into the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP systems were evaluated by the MD simulation runs. Additionally, the temperature effects (25, 35, 45 and 55 °C) were assessed on the FFV, FV, interaction energies, radius of gyration, solubility parameters, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns as well as diffusion coefficients. Results were analyzed to achieve the most appropriate nanocomposite anticancer CP drug delivery system. After finding the best system (CS-GN-CP), polyethylene glycol (PEG) chains and/or vitamin C (VC) molecule were incorporated into the CS-GN-CP cell to study the effects of such biomaterials on the drug delivery efficacy.

The condensed-phase optimized molecular potentials (COMPASS) force field (FF) was utilized in all of the minimization as well as dynamics runs. This FF is often applied for all kinds of structures in atomistic simulations. The COMPASS force field has been based upon the Polymer Consistent Force Field (PCFF) [63,64] that is the first FF parameterized and certified through condensed phase principles as well as empirical and ab initio data determined for numerous compounds. Besides non-bonding and bonding interactions used in other force fields, the cross-coupling interactions are also considered by the COMPASS hence it is able to precisely evaluate various properties of diverse systems and molecules primarily for polymer macromolecules [65,66]. In order to calculate partial atomic charges existing on molecules the charge equilibration approach was chosen [67]. The total energy for a system was obtained by summing non-bond, cross-term and valence interactions. Ewald method was applied to obtain the long range Columbic contacts [68].

All structures were built by means of the molecule modeling tool and their minimizations were done to achieve the structures with the minimum energies. The minimization steps were accomplished 5,000,000 iterations in a cascading route till the relaxation was reached. The cells were created with periodic boundary conditions using amorphous cell module existing in the Materials Studio program [69]. The cells were generated with an initial very low density of 0.20 g/cm3 in order to allow them to be well equilibrated and reach their almost real equilibrium densities. The amorphous cells contained five CS chains as the polymeric matrix containing 20 repeating units, one graphene (G, GN or GP) nanosheet as the filler and three CP drug molecules in order to assess the impacts of these systems loaded with three types of nanosheets (as fillers) on the efficacy of the drug delivery. Table 1 indicates complete compositions of the cells and a summary of the simulation results. After determining the most suitable system (CS-GN-CP), 1–3 polyethylene glycol (PEG) chains and/or one vitamin C (VC) molecule were added into the CS-GN-CP cell in order to evaluate the effects of PEG and VC on the drug delivery performance.

It is known that the water molecules can certainly affect the properties of the drug delivery systems because the drug and carrier species are engaged in the hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions with the H2O molecules [43]. However, the simulation runs are highly time-consuming when water molecules are added into the systems designed in this work. Thus, the MD simulations were carried out without incorporation of H2O molecules. Another reason for accomplishing the MD simulations in absence of water molecules is that we previously investigated the effect of water molecules on the drug delivery efficacy in one of our earlier papers [43] and we found that the diffusion coefficients and mean square displacements of the drug molecules were decreased in presence of the H2O molecules because they were engaged in the H-bonding and electrostatic interactions with the water molecules. Fortunately, the diffusion coefficients and mean square displacements of all systems were decreased to some extent so that an identical trend was observed for the drug diffusion coefficients in both of the systems simulated in presence and absence of H2O molecules [43]. Therefore, we were also able to choose the most suitable drug delivery system without adding water molecules. Inspired by this fact and considering the very much time-consuming MD simulation runs in presence of the H2O molecules, the MD simulations were done in absence of water molecules to find the most appropriate drug delivery system.

The MD simulations were carried out to fully relax the polymeric nanocomposite systems. The NVT simulations were initially performed for 20 ns at a certain temperature and 1 atm and next the NPT simulations were carried out for 20 ns at desired temperature. This methodology could entirely relax the amorphous cells and accelerate to reach their equilibrium densities. The temperature influence on various properties of systems and their drug delivery efficacy were examined through performing the MD runs at four temperatures including 298.15, 308.15, 318.15 and 328.15 K (25, 35, 45 and 55 °C). In the course of the relaxation, the non-bond interactions such as van der Waals as well as electrostatic potentials were estimated by the Ewald approach. Subsequently, NVT simulations were accomplished at the relaxed densities for 3 ns using cell multipole method (CMM). Finally, another last 1 ns simulation was done on each relaxed trajectory that was then utilized to analyze structural, dynamical and energetic characteristics of systems [70]. The velocity Verlet algorithm was applied to employ the classic motion relationship (time step=1 fs) [71]. During the all of the NVT and the NPT simulation runs, the Berendsen thermostat and barostat procedures (decay constant=0.1 ps) were used in to preserve the temperatures and pressure at anticipated values [72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Equilibrium and relaxation of amorphous cells
ce:section.ce:para: To study various features of CS polymer nanocomposites systems composed of graphene nanosheets (G, GN or GP), the equilibrations and relaxations of the cells have been accomplished. Herein, relaxation characteristics like temperature as well as non-bond, potential and kinetic energies are acquired by means of the MD runs. Fig. S1 reveals the chemical structures of CS chain, CP drug as well as the G, GN and GP nanosheets. Fig. 1 presents the relaxed configurations of the polymeric composites obtained at four temperatures. Table 1 provides the cell size (dimension) and density for the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP polymeric nanocomposites.

It is seen that the cell dimension and accordingly the cell volume are enhanced from CS-G-CP to CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP systems and the biggest cell size is achieved for CS-GP-CP cell. The variations in cell size can be associated with the largest size of the P atom doped in the GP nanosheet. The volumes for the G, GN and GP nanosheets are calculated to be 250.99, 252.97 and 255.45 Ǻ3, respectively, confirming the GP species has occupied the utmost volume. Fig. S2 illustrates the structural formulae and volumes (shown by blue color) for the G, GN and GP nanosheets indicating their volumes change as GP>GN>G.

At 25 °C, the density is equal to 1.33 g/cm3 for the CS-G-CP cell, whereas it is enhanced to 1.35 and 1.34 g/cm3 for the CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP nanocomposites, respectively. As a result, the density of these cells varies as CS-G-CP<CS-GP-CP<CS-GN-CP demonstrating the CS-GN-CP has the largest density value and this may be attributed to the variations in the occupied volumes of the polymeric nanocomposite systems because the occupied volume illustrates a reverse relationship with the density. As it is seen in Table 1, analogous data are obtained at higher temperatures for the densities of the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP cells.

A comparison of the densities acquired at diverse temperatures for the CS-G-CP clarifies that a reverse relationship exists between the temperature and density as the density decreases by the temperature enhancement; this result can be due to increasing the occupied volume at higher temperature. The densities are close to those the reported in the literature for the CS nanocomposites, e.g., the densities achieved for the CS were 1.42 [73] and 1.40 g/cm3 [74]. Hence, the calculated density values for such polymer nanocomposite systems are acceptable and this result is regarded as another reason for attaining cell equilibration.

The variations in the non-bond, potential and kinetic energies plus temperature against simulation time for all of the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP polymeric membranes at four temperatures are exhibited in Figs. S3 and S4. Also, Table S1 lists the average non-bond, potential and kinetic energies which indicate the variations are so small confirming the systems have been relaxed. Comparing all kinds of the energies for the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP systems proves that the CS-GN-CP cell has the smallest energies; the CS-GP-CP indicates medium values and the CS-G-CP exhibits the largest energies. As well, the kinetic energies have minimum values among all energies. For example, at 25 °C, the kinetic energies measured for the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP systems are approximately 2180, 1575 and 1870 kcal/mol and their potential energies are ca. 4810, 4210 and 4525 kcal/mol whereas their non-bond energies are nearly 4990, 4415 and 4705 kcal/mol, respectively. As a result, the CS-GN-CP with the lowest energy values may be chosen as the most apposite system. Nevertheless, the most suitable drug carrier cannot be selected only by considering the energies and other properties of the polymeric matrixes must be evaluated. Therefore, the effects of polymeric nanocomposite type and temperature on the system properties and their drug delivery capability are discovered in next sections with the purpose of finding the most appropriate DDS for the CP transport.

Table 1 indicates the surface areas for the CS nanocomposite systems. At 25 °C, the surface areas of the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP systems are 7011.26, 7199.48 and 7310.27 Å2, respectively which reflects the surface areas vary as CS-GP-CP>CS-GN-CP>CS-G-CP. Such result is attributed to the fact that the GP has the largest surface area. A comparable result is obtained for the surface areas of such nanocomposites measured at temperatures higher than 25 °C. Likewise, for all of the cells, the surface area is increased upon the temperature enhancement from 25 to 55 °C. For instance, for the CS-G-CP system at 35, 45 and 55 °C, the surface areas are obtained equal to 7185.38, 7362.69 and 7519.42 Ǻ2, respectively.

Two discrete phases existing in a polymer system include polymeric chains in addition to other materials along with spaces recognized as FV [75]. The easiness of diffusion in a polymer-based matrix is dependent on its morphology as well as FV. The FV value is achieved by summing the static holes (generated through chains packing) and/or transient gaps (produced by thermally reorganized chains) [76]. Typically, the FV directly affects the molecular diffusion in polymeric membranes and a higher FV leads to a greater diffusion [77]. The Connolly surface methodology was used to achieve the FVs for the CS polymer nanocomposite systems. In this approach, a hard sphere (radius=0.1 nm) was used as a probe. The FV values calculated by this method are dependent on the radius of the probe.

Table 1 displays the FV amounts for the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP polymer nanocomposite systems. Furthermore, Fig. 2 depicts the FV values with the blue color for such cells. At 25 °C, the free volume is maximum for the CS-GN-CP nanocomposite (11,962.49 Å3) but it is dropped to 11,733.71 Å3 in CS-GP-CP system and the CS-G-CP cell reveals the smallest FV (11,592.47 Å3). Analogous results are perceived at other temperatures. In addition, the FV value is decreased upon the temperature increase from 25 to 55 °C that can be correlated to the enhanced occupied volumes at higher temperatures.

The fractional free volume (FFV) equals the FV ratio to the total cell volume. At 25 °C, the CS-GN-CP has the greatest FFV (41.84%), the CS-GP-CP presents a medium FFV (41.00%) and the CS-G-CP indicates the minimum FFV (40.59%). The FFVs change like the FV amounts and the data support the results attained for both of the cell volumes and cell sizes. As an example, the FFVs for the CS-GN-CP cell at 35, 45 and 55 °C are 40.85, 40.03 and 39.45%, respectively, which validate the FFV decreases at a higher temperature by increasing the occupied volume. Accordingly, fewer empty spaces are created in the polymer matrix at the higher temperature, which result in smaller FV/FFV amounts.

The utmost FFV for the CS-GN-CP cell may exhibit that the most facile CP drug diffusion can occur within this DDS and it may be estimated that the largest diffusion coefficient is acquired for this system. Smaller FV/FFV values for the CS-G-CP and CS-GP-CP nanocomposites are related to the restricted mobility of polymer chains that are near the polymer–graphene interface that create some nanosized holes as suitable spaces for drug transport/diffusion.

It is recognized that an enhancement in the FV/FFV usually brings about a more effective penetration into the polymer matrix [78]. The relationship of FV and distribution in the polyamide matrix and transport mechanism was investigated by positron annihilation lifetime spectra and it was shown that using the pressurized filtration in all membranes, the rejection rates had a reverse relationship with FV values so that more mass transport was observed in the system with a greater FV [79]. Recently, blend membranes were prepared using polynorbornene as well as 2,2′-bis(3,4′-dicarboxyphenyl)hexafluoropropane dianhydride-durene in order to increase their H2 permselectivity; it was described that some structural variations were happened upon blending that improved the permeability by enhancement of FFV values [80]. As well, it was demonstrated that the separation efficiency of a polyamide membrane was significantly connected to its FV [81].

As the FV/FFV values of the CS-GN-CP system are the greatest among all nanocomposite systems while they are the minimum values for the CS-G-CP, it is expected that maximum CP diffusion rates will be seen in the CS-GN-CP composite. Nonetheless, to achieve the most proper DDS, only considering the free volumes is not correct and further parameters must be evaluated including gyration radii, radial distribution functions, X-ray diffraction patterns and mean square displacements.

The radius of gyration can define the polymer chain dimension. It is measured by the subsequent equation [82], where ri and mi reveal the position and mass vectors of atom i relative to the molecular mass center. The Rg displays the distance measured between the beads and the mass center and a bigger Rg stands for a larger distance between the polymer chain and its mass center [82]. The probability density distributions of the gyration radii plots and the Rg data for all CS polymeric systems are presented in Table 1 and Fig. 3 a, respectively. At 25 °C, it is observed that the CS-GN-CP offers minimum Rg of 2.95 Å, the CS-GP-CP reveals that Rg is 3.04 Å and the CS-G-CP has the maximum Rg of 3.08 Å. Comparable data are attained at higher temperatures. Hence, it can be concluded that the polymer chains inside the CS-GN-CP nanocomposite are in the closest contacts and have the greatest intermolecular interactions that have resulted in their extreme vicinity. This leads to the largest FVs and FFVs for the CS-GN-CP among all of the nanocomposite systems.

Besides, the Rg is increased as a result of the temperature raising, e.g., the Rg amounts of the CS-GN-CP cell at 35, 45 and 55 °C are 3.00, 3.05 and 3.11 Å, respectively. Consequently, raising the temperature can cause easier motions of polymer chains and accordingly it results in a greater Rg. Therefore, it can be concluded that the CP delivery can be the most effective in the CS-GN-CP cell with the uppermost FV which can provide the most expedited CP diffusion. To compare the results with experimentally reported values, it was shown that the Rg for the CS was 5.25 Å for the unperturbed chains [83]. It was found that the Rg of the CS was obtained as 2.7 Å [84]. The Rg also depends on the polymerization degree so that in case of the CS polymer with polymerization degrees of 646 to 2100, the Rg was altered in the range of 3.44 to 8.25 Å [85].

The XRD patterns of all DDSs are picked up using the scattering analysis in the Forcite module. The diffraction angle changes in the range of 0–120° with a step size of 0.05° The d-spacing values illuminating inter-layer distances between polymer chains were calculated using the most intese peaks in the XRD patterns.

The XRD patterns for the CS polymer nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 3b. The XRD graphs reveal the material's crystallinity by comparing the intensities of peaks. Crystalline compounds indicate intense peaks while amorphous materials such as glasses and liquids depict broad signal(s). The XRD patterns in Fig. 3b illustrate one sharp peak near 10° plus a broad peak close to 20° which confirm the occurrence of CS polymer, G/GN/GP nanosheet and CP drug in all cells. Analogous results were presented for the graphene that exhibited a characteristic peak in the XRD pattern at ~23° [88]. Chitosan reveals two peaks in the XRD pattern at around 10 and 20°. This is in consistent with the literature reported patterns [89]. It is noteworthy that the CS crystallinity is related to existing intermolecular H-bonds formed between its N-acetyl, hydroxyl and amino groups which create a sharp peak in its XRD diagram that is produced by anti-parallel as well as parallel alignments of polymer chains. Both crystalline and amorphous regions were observed in the XRD diagram of the semi-crystalline CS [89]. Additionally, at 25 °C, the XRD pattern of a non-neutralized chitosan demonstrated a broad peak near 18° accompanied by two intense peaks at around 9 and 11° which reflected it was a semi-crystalline material [90].

It is apparent that the CS-GN-CP has the lowest intensities of peaks whereas the intensities are enhanced in the CS-G-CP and CS-GP-CP cells demonstrating nearly equal intensities. This confirms that the CS-GN-CP has the lowest crystallinity while other systems have higher crystallinities. As well, at 25 °C, the position of the peak at ~10° shifts to lower values from CS-GN-CP (12.32°) to the CS-GP-CP (11.46°) and CS-G-CP (10.15°). This is attributed to the lowest inter-chain distance measured for the CS-GN-CP cell while the largest amount is attained for the CS-G-CP. Comparable data are achieved at elevated temperatures. Also, the temperature raise can decrease the diffraction angle. As an example, the diffraction angles of the CS-GN-CP nanocomposite at 25, 35, 45 and 55 °C are 12.32, 11.64, 10.95 and 10.47°, respectively.

The Bragg equation, λ=2dsinθ, was applied to calculate the inter-chain distances. Table 1 affords the 2θ values for the intensive peaks in the XRD patterns and their corresponding d-spacings of the CS nanocomposite systems. At 25 °C, the inter-chain distances of the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP are measured to be 0.8708, 0.7179 and 0.7715 Å, respectively. Similar data are achieved at elevated temperatures. The decrease in the d-spacing could be associated with superior intermolecular interactions occurred through the creation of weak and strong H-bonds among CS polymeric chains, G/GN/GP nanosheet and the CP drug. Such results support the FV/FFV data as the CS-GN-CP exhibits the biggest FV/FFV values among the systems that is caused by the utmost inter-particle interactions.

To study the interaction distances inside polymer nanocomposite systems, the RDF (also known as pair correlation function), gA–B(r), has been calculated. The RDF identifies the likely existence of B atoms around A atoms. The gA–B(r) is calculated mathematically by the following formula in which nB stands for the number of B atoms located nearby A atoms within a spherical shell having the Δr thickness, NB shows the total number of B atoms and V reveals the system volume.
g
A − − B
( r )
=
(
n B
4 π
r 2
Δ
r
)
(
N B
V
)

Different RDF values specify strong, moderate and weak interactions happened among particles. Figs. 4-6 illuminate RDF graphs at diverse temperatures obtained for the most significant inter-atomic interactions took place within the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP as well as CS-GP-CP cells. It should be noted that the N(endo-CP)–H(OH-CS) displays the distance measured for the interaction of the endocyclic nitrogen atom of CP with the hydrogen atom on the hydroxyl group of CS. Such explanation is applied to all of RDFs provided in Figs. 4–6
. In addition, the RDF distances achieved for the intermolecular interactions of CS chains, G/GN/GP nanosheet and CP drug molecules in three systems are presented in Tables S2-S4.

It is found that the strongest interactions at four temperatures inside CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP systems include drug-polymer, moderate interactions are graphene-polymer and the graphene-drug are the weakest interactions demonstrating the strengths of these interactions vary as graphene-drug<graphene-polymer<drug-polymer. This is recognized as the strongest electrostatic and hydrogen bond interactions happened among the functional groups of the CS polymeric chains and the CP drug molecules. There are not any functional groups on the pristine graphene therefore it cannot very strongly interact with the CS polymer chains and the CP molecules. Consequently, the graphene-drug and the graphene-polymer interactions are weaker compared to those measured for the drug-polymer interactions.

Comparing the graphene-drug and graphene-polymer interactions verifies that the latter are much stronger and this is associated with the presence of more functional groups on the CS polymer chains relative to those of the CP drug molecules which bring about stronger graphene-polymer interactions. For instance, at 25 °C, the O(P=O)-H(OH-CS) RDF in the CS-G-CP cell is 2.27 Å that is identified as the drug-polymer interaction but the O(P=O)-C(G) and H(OH-CS)-C(G) RDFs are 3.65 and 2.31 Å known as the graphene-drug and the graphene-polymer interactions, respectively. The O(P=O)-H(OHCS), O(P=O)-C(GN) and H(OH-CS)-C(GN) distances at 25 °C are 2.17, 9.03 and 2.28 Å for the CS-GN-CP, respectively. At 25 °C, the distances for the O(P=O)-H(OHCS), O(P=O)-C(GP) and H(OHCS)-C(GP) interactions are 2.20, 7.81 and 2.33 Å in the CS-GP-CP cell, respectively. Such results support the above-mentioned data indicating the strongest interactions occur among CS polymeric chains and CP drug. Analogous results are attained at elevated temperatures, too. Nonetheless, the RFDs are enhanced upon the temperature increase and this can be correlated to the facilitated motions of all components, i.e. the CS polymer chains, CP molecules and G/GN/GP nanosheet at higher temperature leading to bigger RDFs for the interacting atoms.

Comparing the graphene-polymer interactions happened among the functional groups of the CS chains and the GN carbon/nitrogen atoms discloses that much stronger interactions occur with the nitrogen atom of the GN nanosheet relative to those taking place with its C atoms. Similarly, the interactions with the GP phosphorus atom are very stronger compared to the ones with its C atom. The RDF values for the O(P=O)-C(GN), O(P=O)-N(GN) as well as O(P=O)-C(GP), O(P=O)-P(GP) interactions at 25 °C are measured to be 9.03, 2.61 Å as well as 7.81, 3.01 Å, respectively. Similar data are attained for the graphene-drug interactions. In addition, the RDF distances in Tables S3 and S4 demonstrate that stronger interactions take place with the GN nitrogen atom than those with GP phosphorus atom thus larger RDFs are measured for the latter. This is described as the higher electronegativity of the nitrogen atom than that of the phosphorus atom, which causes stronger electrostatic and hydrogen bond interactions.

It has been indicated that the strongest interactions in all systems take place among the drug and polymer and the smallest drug-polymer distances are measured for the CS-GN-CP, moderate values belong to the CS-GP-CP cell and CS-G-CP composite exhibits the largest distances. Such observations approve that the strongest H-bond interactions happen within the CS-GN-CP while the weakest interactions occur inside the CS-G-CP system. Hence, it is obvious, according to the RDF values, that the diffusion of the CP molecules takes place the most easily within the CS-GN-CP cell, moderately in the CS-GP-CP but the most weakly in the CS-G-CP. Also, the drug diffusion is increased at higher temperatures due to its weaker interactions with other species. As a result, it may be predicted that the CS-GN-CP is the most promising drug delivery vehicle.

The solubility parameter (δ) numerically estimates the power of interactions occurred among compounds (mostly for polymers) specifying their solubility [91]. It is applied to calculate the swelling, compatibility, bulk and/or solution properties and permeation for polymers [91]. It is the square root of cohesive energy density (ECED) for a pure material according to the Hildebrand-Scatchard solution theory, see Eq. (1).
(1)
δ =
E
C E D

The ECED is the energy which is necessary to completely eliminate the molecular unit volume from its neighbors into the infinite space as an ideal gas. This equals the ΔHvap–RT divided by molar volume (V), see Eq. (2), where ΔHvap shows the molar vaporization enthalpy, T indicates the absolute temperature, R points to the universal gas constant and E stands for the vaporization energy.
(2)
δ =
Δ
H
v a p
− R T
V
=
E V

The Hildebrand parameter is composed of three Hansen parameters that are hydrogen bond, dispersion and polar forces shown as δh, δd and δp solubility parameters, respectively [92]. The solubility parameter is a thermophysical property for a material which highly depends on the temperature.
(3)
δ =
δ d 2
+
δ h 2
+
δ p 2

The solubility parameter values for the CS nanocomposites and all of their constituent components calculated at diverse temperatures are given in Table 2 . The δ values in all cases are diminished upon temperature increasing which is related to the enhanced molecular volumes at elevated temperatures that can lessen the δ (see Eq. (3)). It is distinguished that though the dispersion energy at a higher temperature is the greatest energy, the volume increase is predominant. Likewise, Table 2 exhibits that the δ values for the CP, G, GN and GP are very comparable to that of the CS signifying such compounds are very compatible with each other and also they can properly be miscible. A comparison of the solubility parameters of the G, GN and GP nanosheets displays that the GN affords the highest value while the GP has the lowest δ; e.g., at 25 °C, the solubility parameter values of the G, GN and GP are 14.953, 15.268 and 14.306 (J.cm−3)0.5, respectively. The largest δ value for the GN is associated with the utmost electronegativity of nitrogen among carbon, nitrogen and phosphorus elements which can cause the strongest H-bond and polar (dipole) interactions. On the other hand, the least solubility parameter for the GP can be correlated to its biggest volume leading to the smallest δ value according to Eq. (2). The CS-G-CP and CS-GN-CP cells illustrate the lowest and supreme solubility parameters, respectively, and the CS-GP-CP exhibits a medium amount. As an example, the solubility parameters calculated at 25 °C for the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP systems are 24.018, 26.338 and 25.028 (J.cm−3)0.5, respectively. The smallest δ amount acquired for the CS-G-CP cell is attributed to the lack of any functional groups on the pristine G particle which does not strongly interact with the CP and CS as established by the RDF values. The maximum solubility parameters for the CS-GN-CP system proves that the strongest intermolecular interactions take place inside this polymer nanocomposite matrix which bring about the slowest and the most controlled delivery of drug molecules. Therefore, according to the solubility parameter data, the suitability order of three drug delivery systems is CS-G-CP<CS-GP-CP<CS-G-CP.

The molecular diffusion of the CP drug within the CS polymeric nanocomposite systems has been obtained through the MSD plots, see Fig. 7 . The approximately linear lines obtained in the MSD diagrams depict constant drug diffusion into the nanocomposite cells throughout the simulations [93]. The CS-GN-CP system affords the lowest MSD for the CP diffusion, the CS-GP-CP reveals medium drug diffusion but the CS-G-CP exhibits the highest one. Additionally, in all of the DDSs, greater MSD values are achieved at higher temperatures. For an efficient delivery of the CP drug, the system must show the lowest MSD. Accordingly, the CS-GN-CP system is used to have the slowest and the most controlled CP diffusion/transport. Fig. 7 evidently exhibits that the CS-GN-CP is the most effective and suitable cell for the sustained transport of the CP drug.

The diffusion coefficients of the drug molecules into the three CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP nanocomposite systems were measured at four different temperatures using the slopes of the MSD graphs (Table 2). The diffusion coefficients data in Table 2 are in agreement with the MSD plots because the diffusion coefficient is increased by raising the temperature. For instance, at 55 °C, the lowest diffusion coefficient is recognized for the CS-GN-CP (0.0553 × 10–5 cm2/s), the CS-GP-CP demonstrates a medium value of 0.0595 × 10–4 cm2/s and the CS-G-CP reveals the highest CP diffusion coefficient of 0.0658 × 10–4 cm2/s. As a result, the slowest drug diffusion occurs into the CS-GN-CP which can lead to the most controlled and proficient drug delivery. Consequently, the capacity of the DDSs to deliver the CP drug alters as CS-G-CP<CS-GP-CP<CS-GN-CP. The reason is related to the existence of nitrogen and phosphorus elements doped into the graphene nanosheets in the CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP systems whereas the pristine graphene has been added to the CS-G-CP. The presence of the GN and the GP functionalized graphene nanosheets result in their more effective electrostatic and H-bond interactions with the CS polymer chains and the CP drug. Consequently, the drug diffusion happens more efficiently (controlled) in such systems relative to the CS-G-CP comprising pure G.

Finally, the CS-GN-CP can be selected as the most appropriate DDS and the temperature 35 °C was recognized as the most suitable temperature because not only this is close to the human body temperature but also the CS-GN-CP reveals favorable CP diffusion coefficient as well as FV, FFV values at this temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of adding PEG and vitamin C (VC)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the influence of incorporating PEG chains and vitamin C molecule on the drug delivery capacity of the CS-GN-CP as the most suitable system, the MD simulations were run at 25 and 35 °C on the CS-GN-CP cells containing 1–3 PEG chains and/or one VC particle. Figs. 8 and S5 exhibit the relaxed configurations of the CS-GN-CP-PEG and CS-GN-CP-PEG-VC systems at 35 and 25 °C, respectively. Some MD simulations results attained at two temperatures (25 and 35 °C) and 1 atm are provided in Table S5. In these systems, both of the cell volumes and the cell sizes are enhanced upon adding VC and more PEG chains into the cells. The density of the CS-GN-CP-PEG1 is 1.32 g/cm3 at 25 °C but it is decreased to 1.30 g/cm3 at 35 °C. The decreased density at the higher temperature can be due to the increased occupied volume. In addition, adding VC and more PEG chains into the system, drops the density to smaller values.

The surface areas become greater at the elevated temperature and this is due to the enhanced occupied volumes. Also, bigger occupied volumes measured at 35 °C (higher temperature) decrease the FV and FFV values. For instance, at 25 and 35 °C, the FV and FFV amounts of the CS-GN-CP-PEG1 are 10,739.16 Å3, 37.48% and 10,534.08 Å3, 36.76%, respectively. Moreover, the FV and FFV values are decreased by incorporation of VC and more PEG chains into the cells. Fig. S6 displays the free volumes (indicated by blue color) for the CS-GN-CP-PEG1, CS-GN-CP-PEG1-VC, CS-GN-CP-PEG2, CS-GN-CP-PEG2-VC, CS-GN-CP-PEG3 and CS-GN-CP-PEG3-VC drug delivery systems obtained at 35 °C. As an example, at 35 °C, the FVs and FFVs of the CS-GNCP-PEG1-VC and CS-GN-CP-PEG2 are 10,308.74 Å3, 35.87% and 9189.88 Å3, 31.92%, respectively.

The kinetic, non-bond and potential energy diagrams against simulation time at 35 and 25 °C for the CS-GN-CP-PEG and CS-GN-CP-PEG-VC systems are presented in Figs. S7 and S8, respectively. Furthermore, Table S6 reveals average values of non-bond, kinetic and potential energies for these systems. It is observed that all of the energies are enlarged at the higher temperature. Also, adding VC and more PEG chains into the cells enhances the energies so that the CS-GN-CP-PEG system displays the least non-bond, kinetic and potential energies but the greatest values are achieved for the CS-GN-CP-PEG3-VC cell. For example, at 25 °C, the non-bond, potential and kinetic energies for the CS-GN-CP-PEG system are 2180, 4550 and 4880 kcal/mol but they are 5120, 4830 and 2560 kcal/mol, respectively.

The CP diffusion coefficients measured at 25 and 35 °C for the GN-CP-PEG and CS-GN-CP-PEG-VC systems are listed in Table S7. As well, Fig. S9 illustrates the MSD graphs for the CP drug diffusion in the polymer nanocomposites. The diffusion coefficients are increased at the higher temperature but they are decreased by adding VC and more PEG chains to the systems. For comparison, for the GN-CP-PEG1 system, the diffusion coefficients at 25 and 35 °C are 0.0347 × 10–5 and 0.0383 × 10–5 cm2/s but they are 0.0307 × 10–5 and 0.0342 × 10–5 cm2/s, respectively, for the CS-GNCP-PEG1-VC. The smallest diffusion coefficient is achieved at 25 °C for the CS-GN-CP-PEG3-VC (0.0180 × 10–5 cm2/s).

In conclusion, in order to choose the most suitable drug delivery system, it must be noted that the desired system has a high drug loading amount in addition to a sustained drug release. As it is observed in Table S7, the diffusion coefficients of the CP drug in systems CS-GN-CP-PEG2-VC and CS-GN-CP-PEG3-VC are close to each other but it is a little smaller in the latter. Therefore, the CS-GN-CP-PEG2-VC system with a higher CP drug loading capacity can be selected as the most appropriate drug carrier. Also, the system working CS-GN-CP-PEG2-VC working at 35 °C is known as the most favorable system as this temperature is close to the human body temperature.

MD simulations were conducted at diverse temperatures (25, 35, 45 and 55 °C) on the CS-G-CP, CS-GN-CP and CS-GP-CP polymer nanocomposites that contained G, GN or GP nanosheets with the aim of assessing the most promising delivery system for the CP drug. At any temperature, the CS-GN-CP had the utmost FFV amounts, the CS-GP-CP exhibited medium FFVs and the CS-G-CP illustrated minimum values confirming the simplest CP drug diffusion/transport may occur in the CS-GN-CP. At any temperature, minimum Rg amounts were calculated for the CS-GN-CP system, medium Rg values were achieved for the CS-GP-CP and the maximum values were displayed by the CS-G-CP. Such results ascertained that the CS polymer chains inside the CS-GN-CP showed the closest intermolecular interactions and nearest contacts with each other that caused their utmost vicinity leading to the maximum FVs and FFVs for the CS-GN-CP cell among others. All nanocomposites demonstrated one high intensity peak near 10° plus a broad peak about 20° in their XRD patterns which established the occurrence of the CS chains, G/GN/GP nanosheets and CP drug in the systems. The RDF values clarified that in all systems, the strongest, medium and the weakest interactions were drug-polymer, graphene-polymer and graphene-drug interactions, respectively. Furthermore, the RDF data proved that the CP drug molecules exhibited the strongest interaction with other particles in the CS-GN-CP nanocomposite but they moderately and the weakest interacted within the CS-GP-CP and CS-G-CP systems, respectively, which certified the most simple CP diffusion could happen in the CS-G-CP cell. The CS-G-CP illustrated the greatest diffusion coefficient whereas the CS-GP-CP and CS-GN-CP displayed medium and the smallest diffusion coefficients, respectively, confirming the most controlled/efficient drug delivery was occurred in the CS-GN-CP. In the next step, PEG chains and VC molecule were incorporated into the CS-GN-CP system with the purpose of evaluating the influences of these biomaterials on the system capability for the drug delivery. Finally, the CS-GN-CP-PEG2-VC working at 308.15 K (close to the human body temperature) was selected as the most desirable drug delivery system as it had a relatively high drug loading capacity and displayed suitable CP diffusion coefficient and FV, FFV values.
